root.title: Cúram 8.1.2 Child Services Business Guide
root.summary: This section is the full front matter and main introductory content for the Cúram 8.1.2 Child Services Business Guide. It introduces CCS for child welfare agencies and explains its purpose, audience, and background. Key topics include supporting caseworkers, intake/referral, investigation, removal and return, outcome management, ongoing case management, adoption, performance indicators, and scenario-based walkthroughs. It also covers caseworker tools such as home pages, prior history, common intake, correspondence, contacts, multidisciplinary/legal actions, eligibility/background information, and inter-jurisdictional handling. The included scenarios illustrate child protection, family services, information and referral, inter-jurisdictional placement, and adoption workflows. The end of the section contains legal notices, privacy policy, trademarks, copyright, and sample-code disclaimers.

children.title: Note
children.summary: Introductory note for the document. It instructs readers to review the Notices section on page 43 before using the information or the product it supports. It also includes the Merative US L.P. copyright notice (2012, 2024) on pages 3–4.

children.title: Edition
Edition — pages 5-14
children.summary: Edition page for Cúram/CCS 8.1, 8.1.1, and 8.1.2, followed by the table of contents and the start of the Child Services Business Guide. Introduces Child Services goals, audience, and background in child welfare. Covers major processes: supporting the caseworker, intake/referral, investigation, removal/return, outcome management, ongoing case management, adoption, performance indicators, and scenarios. Also describes home pages/pods, prior history view, common intake, correspondence, and managing contacts.

children.title: Edition — pages 15-24
children.summary: This section covers child welfare contact logs and intake processing. It explains recording, editing, appending, and searching contacts; narrative history and editable periods; search operators and contact limits; and upcoming/overdue contacts on the home page. It also introduces multidisciplinary team collaboration and shared information. The latter part describes CCS intake: capturing reporter/participant details, narrative, intake types (CPS, FS, IJ, I&R), screening and response recommendations, allegations, and child protection/family services intake details.

children.title: Edition — pages 25-34
children.summary: This section outlines child services case workflows from intake through investigation, removal, placement, ongoing management, and adoption. It covers inter-jurisdictional intake and agreements, screening and investigation steps (contacting family, assessing safety/risk, disposing allegations, determining response), removal types and provider/placement concepts, outcome and visitation plan monitoring, and ongoing case management. It also explains adoption case creation, recruitment, pre-adoptive conferences, placement recommendations and decisions, and adoption subsidy support.

children.title: Edition — pages 35-42
children.summary: This section describes Cúram performance indicators and dashboards for child welfare reporting, including operational measures like initial contact timelines and ongoing contact timeliness, plus licensed dashboards such as recurrence of maltreatment and permanency. It then presents detailed scenario-based workflows across child protection, family services, information and referral, inter-jurisdictional placement, and adoption. The scenarios illustrate intake, assessment, safety decisions, ongoing case management, MDT planning, and legal actions using named participants and case examples.

Introduces the Child Services Business Guide for Cúram/CCS 8.1–8.1.2. Covers child welfare goals, audience, and background, then traces core workflows: supporting caseworkers, intake/referral, investigation, removal/return, outcome management, ongoing case management, adoption, and performance indicators. Also includes home pages, prior history, common intake, correspondence, contacts, MDT/legal actions, and inter-jurisdictional handling. Ends with scenario-based examples for child protection, family services, information and referral, inter-jurisdictional placement, and adoption.

children.title: Notices
Privacy policy
children.summary: Merative’s privacy policy is referenced here, with a link to the full policy at https://www.merative.com/privacy. This page also contains legal notices: sample code is provided “as is” under the Merative Client Agreement, may be copied for API-related development, and carries no warranty or liability. It also lists Merative and third-party trademarks and copyright information.

children.title: Trademarks
children.summary: Legal notices for the document: fictionalized example names, sample code copyright and “as is” warranty disclaimer, privacy policy link, and trademark attributions. Lists Merative, IBM, Adobe, Oracle/Java, Linux, Microsoft/Windows, and UNIX as trademarks or registered trademarks, with a note that other names may belong to others. Includes Merative copyright years 2012 and 2024.

Legal notices and usage terms for the publication: personal and enterprise copying is allowed only if proprietary notices are preserved, but redistribution, derivative works, and external use require Merative’s consent. It disclaims warranties (“as-is”), limits rights/licensing, reserves withdrawal of permissions, and requires export-law compliance. It also notes no endorsement or validation of third-party products or websites, and includes patent, copyright (2012, 2024), and trademark information.

children.title: Contents
children.summary: Table of contents for the Cúram 8.1.2 Child Services Business Guide. Lists major sections and page locations covering Child Services Overview, Supporting the Caseworker, Intake, Investigation, Removal to Return, Outcome Management, Ongoing Case Management, Adoption, Performance Indicators, and Scenario examples. Also includes front matter and legal notices such as Note, Edition, Privacy policy, and Trademarks. Useful as a navigation index rather than substantive content.

children.title: 1 Child Services Business Guide
1.1 Child Services Overview
Supporting the Caseworker
children.summary: Overview of Cúram caseworker support features for child welfare case management. Describes configurable home pages, prior history view, common intake, correspondence, contact management, multidisciplinary team collaboration, legal action tracking, background information, and special cautions. Also notes budget/expenditure tracking and funding/eligibility determinations for placements, services, and items. Ends with an introduction to referral/intake, including screening abuse/neglect reports and capturing narrative, injury, child/family, witness, location, and safety information.

children.title: Referral to Recommendation (Intake)
children.summary: This section explains Cúram’s intake and caseworker support features. It covers tools such as home pages, prior history view, common intake, correspondence, contact management, MDT collaboration, legal action tracking, background information, and special cautions. It also notes budget/expenditure tracking and eligibility-based funding codes. The “Referral to Recommendation (Intake)” part describes child welfare intake: receiving and screening abuse/neglect reports, capturing narrative, injuries, child/caregiver details, witnesses, and safety/location information to decide whether intervention is needed.

children.title: Recommendation to Response(Investigation)
children.summary: This section describes the child welfare investigation process after a report of abuse or neglect. It covers intake reporting, prompt recording of allegations, face-to-face interviews, gathering information from other sources, coordination with law enforcement, safety planning, and risk assessment. It also explains removal from unsafe homes, court involvement, kin/foster placement, inter-jurisdictional cooperation, and the goal of reunification. Includes related system actions for recording home removal and placement dates, ending prior placements, and returning the child home.

children.title: Removal to Return
children.summary: Explains the removal-to-return workflow in child welfare. When a child is unsafe at home, caseworkers may remove the child after other safety options are tried, with court involvement and possible kinship or inter-jurisdictional placements. The agency’s goal is safe reunification. It also describes how staff record a home removal, place the child with a provider, end or update placements when the child moves, and mark return home when applicable.

children.title: Outcome Management
children.summary: This section explains Outcome Management in Cúram child welfare: it supports assessment, planning, progress measurement, reassessment, and reporting to legal authorities. It is configurable to agency practice models and assessment tools, and integrates visitation planning for out-of-home placements. It also describes ongoing case management as the operational case process after investigation, covering contacts, legal activities, placement management, and coordination with children, families, and MDTs.

children.title: Ongoing Case Management
children.summary: Describes ongoing case management for child welfare cases after investigation, including managing contacts, provider interactions, legal activities, placement management, and regular communication with the child and family. It emphasizes collaboration with MDTs, coordinated service delivery, and reporting planning progress to legal authorities. It also links ongoing case work to outcome plans and other case management activities, supporting operational and logistical case oversight.

children.title: Adoption
children.summary: This section introduces Adoption in child welfare: when reunification is no longer feasible, adoption becomes an option under local jurisdiction practice, with agencies identifying adoptive families and guiding placement and the adoption process. It also outlines performance indicators for safety, well-being, permanency, and worker interactions. The section begins CCS caseworker support content, describing role-based home pages and pods, including CCS-specific pods like “Intakes to be Completed” and “Initial Contact Due” for tracking required casework actions.

children.title: Performance Indicators
children.summary: Defines performance indicators for child welfare: measures of child safety, well-being, permanency, and worker interactions that support these goals. Also introduces CCS support for caseworkers through role-based home pages and pods, including customizable layouts and CCS-specific pods like “Intakes to be Completed” and “Initial Contact Due” for intake workers and investigators.

children.title: Scenarios
children.summary: Introduces child welfare adoption context and explains that the section’s scenarios show how child welfare agencies, caseworkers, children, and families interact with CCS. It also begins “Supporting the Caseworker,” describing CCS home pages tailored by role, with configurable pods that display summary data in list/chart/graphic formats. Examples include “Intakes to be Completed” for intake workers and “Initial Contact Due” for investigators, highlighting pending intakes and due/overdue contacts.

Overview of Child Services (CCS) for agencies protecting children and supporting permanency. Covers the caseworker support tools: configurable home pages, prior history, common intake, correspondence, contacts, MDT collaboration, legal action tracking, background info, special cautions, budgeting/expenditures, and funding/eligibility. Describes intake of abuse/neglect reports and data capture, then investigation, safety planning, removal/placement and reunification workflows, including home removal and placement date management. Also introduces outcome management and ongoing case management after investigation.

children.title: 1.2 Supporting the Caseworker
Home Pages
Intakes to be Completed
children.summary: Describes CCS role-based home page pods. The intake worker’s “Intakes to be Completed” pod highlights intakes needing additional information before completion or with mandatory information entered but not yet submitted. Also explains that CCS home pages use configurable pods to surface key caseworker information and includes an example of the investigator’s “Initial Contact Due” pod for tracking required contacts and due/overdue timeframes.

children.title: Initial Contacts Due
children.summary: Explains the investigator home page pod “Initial Contact Due.” It lists required contacts for alleged abuse victims and shows each contact’s due status, including days until due or overdue. Useful for understanding how CCS helps investigators track mandated initial interviews and prioritize follow-up actions.

children.title: My Open Investigations
children.summary: Describes the investigator’s “My Open Investigations” home-page pod, which lists unfinished investigations needing further action for easy access. The page also briefly covers related caseworker home-page pods such as “Contacts Due,” configurable contact timeframes, capturing contact logs directly from pods, prior history views for participants, Common Intake, correspondence templates, and managing contacts. Useful if you need home-page workflow widgets, investigation follow-up, or related case/contact management features.

children.title: Contact Due
children.summary: Describes the caseworker home page’s “Contacts Due” pod, which lists required child care contacts with due/overdue timeframes based on agency policy. It supports ongoing caseworkers and adoption workers by highlighting required visits and lets users create contact logs for one or more participants directly from the pod without first opening the case. Useful for finding workflow support around monitoring and recording required contacts.

CCS home pages are role-based personal dashboards with configurable pods for caseworkers and supervisors. Pods can show lists, charts, or other views and can be rearranged, added, or hidden to support frequent tasks. CCS includes predefined pods like My Appointments plus CCS-specific pods such as Intakes to be Completed for intake workers and Initial Contact Due for investigators, which track required contacts and due/overdue timeframes. Useful for home-page navigation, workflow prioritization, and pod-based case management.

children.title: Prior History View
children.summary: This section describes the Prior History View, which lets an investigator or caseworker quickly see a person’s previous involvement with the child welfare or child protection agency. Accessed through the citizen context viewer of a case participant, it provides a brief thumbnail sketch of prior history, especially prior abuse or neglect investigations, to support fast decision-making under tight timeframes.

children.title: Common Intake
children.summary: Common Intake describes a configurable intake process across program areas, shaped by agency and local policies. It supports the “no wrong door” approach to help families quickly access needed benefits and services. CCS provides access to Common Intake to assist families in applying for multiple assistance programs. This section is useful if you need details on intake workflow, policy-driven configuration, or multi-program application support.

children.title: Correspondence
children.summary: This section explains Cúram correspondence features for creating Microsoft Word, Pro-forma, and email communications linked to cases or participants. It notes preconfigured templates for common child welfare notices, including Mandated Reporter Intake Letter, Information and Referral Print, Alleged Maltreater Disposition Report, Parent Disposition Report, and Mandated Reporter Disposition Letter. It also begins the related topic of managing contacts, where caseworkers use contact logs to document interactions, actions, learnings, and conclusions.

children.title: Managing Contacts
children.summary: Explains how caseworkers manage contact logs: recording, editing, searching, previewing, printing, and deleting contacts, plus adding participants, attachments, and documents. Covers configurable agency timeframes for required contacts, contact due lists, and access from home-page pods. Describes narratives in detail: append-only history, editable periods, pencil indicators, and who can edit or append. Also covers search by participants, location, purpose, method, date range, subject/narrative text, and OR/AND/NOT operators.

children.title: Multidisciplinary Team Approach
children.summary: Child welfare services are delivered through multidisciplinary teams spanning agencies, community organizations, courts, health, education, housing, and family members. The section explains how MDTs share assessment information, identify safety risks and family needs, and support integrated, family-centered decision making. It also describes the Social Enterprise Folder (SEF) as a secure collaborative space for sharing client and case information. Finally, it notes the Legal Actions Framework, which supports communication and information exchange with legal authorities when court or council involvement is required.

children.title: Legal Actions Framework
children.summary: Describes the Legal Actions Framework as the part of the solution used when legal authority (such as a council, magistrate, or court) is involved in child welfare cases. It emphasizes cooperative, accurate communication and information exchange with legal authorities to support safeguarding children’s well-being. The framework is integrated into business processes when legal involvement is required.

children.title: Eligibility
Title IV-E
children.summary: Explains Title IV-E eligibility in the U.S. Social Security Act for federal reimbursement of foster care and out-of-home placement costs. Describes a sample “Eligibility Determination Process” using rules and evidence such as child age, deprivation, citizenship, and home removal information. Notes that eligible children placed with licensed providers may qualify for Foster Care Maintenance and Adoption Assistance. Also mentions required court-order language (Best Interest, Reasonable Efforts, Permanency) as supported but not included in the sample implementation.

children.title: Other Eligibility Initiatives
children.summary: Describes additional eligibility and child welfare data features in Cúram CCS. It explains how fund-account jurisdictions can use Common Evidence to support automated eligibility determinations and track expenditures against appropriate funding sources. Also covers background information available for children and families, including special cautions, physical characteristics, medical information, educational information, gang affiliations, and account transactions. Special cautions can flag safety/health risks and appear as icons; physical characteristics are stored point-in-time; medical records include immunizations, conditions, disabilities, and allergies.

Explains how the solution’s eligibility feature allocates child/family costs to funding sources based on rules and evidence. Focuses on Title IV-E in the U.S., which determines federal reimbursement eligibility for foster care and other out-of-home placements, including Foster Care Maintenance and Adoption Assistance for eligible children with licensed providers. Describes a sample eligibility determination process using age, deprivation, citizenship, and home removal evidence. Notes court-order requirements (Best Interest, Reasonable Efforts, Permanency) are relevant but not included in the sample.

children.title: Background Information
Special Cautions
children.summary: This section explains “Special Cautions” in CCS: agency-configurable caution flags for individuals that highlight safety-related issues for the child or others, such as runaway risk, suicide risk, allergies, contagious disease, special dietary needs, or violent/sexual offender history. Active cautions display an icon on relevant pages and are referenced in the Cúram Participant Guide. The page also introduces nearby background topics like physical characteristics and medical information.

children.title: Physical Characteristics
children.summary: Explains CCS background information for child welfare users, focusing on record-keeping for children and families. This section highlights special cautions, physical characteristics, medical information, educational information, gang affiliations, and account transactions. The Physical Characteristics subsection says Cúram can store a person’s physical traits as point-in-time records because some attributes (like height and weight) change over time, and the measurement units are configurable in CCS administration.

children.title: Medical Information
children.summary: This section explains that CCS tracks child and family background information relevant to child welfare, including special cautions, physical characteristics, medical information, educational information, gang affiliations, and account transactions. It also notes that agencies using fund-account eligibility models can leverage Cúram’s Common Evidence feature for child welfare-specific eligibility rules. The Medical Information subsection states CCS maintains children’s medical history to support safety and well-being, including immunization history, current and past medical conditions, disabilities, and allergies.

children.title: Educational Information
children.summary: Covers child education tracking in child welfare: school participation, attendance, performance metrics (GPA, assessments, report cards), progress by subject, special needs, recommendations, and storing education documents as attachments (report cards, plans, test results). Also briefly notes gang affiliation tracking for safety/context, and account transactions for bank debits/credits. The excerpt ends as the guide transitions into “Referral to Recommendation (Intake)” describing intake reports/referrals for children in need of services or protection.

children.title: Gang Affiliation
children.summary: This section explains why recording a youth’s gang affiliation matters for case management and safety. It notes that tracking gang membership and start/end dates helps caseworkers understand context, identify related affiliated persons, and make decisions about housing youth together or placing them in group therapy or work programs.

children.title: Account Transactions
children.summary: Defines account transactions as debits and credits recorded against a specified bank account. The solution manages bank account details such as account type and account number, and uses transaction tracking to monitor funds in an individual’s account that may cover services the agency cannot provide or support.

Background Information explains CCS child welfare record types and related eligibility support. It lists key areas tracked for children and families: special cautions, physical characteristics, medical information, educational information, gang affiliations, and account transactions. Special cautions flag safety risks like runaway, suicide, allergies, contagious disease, or violent/sexual offender history and can show an icon when active. Physical traits are stored as point-in-time records with configurable units. The section also notes medical history tracking and mentions Cúram Common Evidence for fund-account eligibility rules.

CCS supports caseworkers with role-based home pages and pods for due contacts, open investigations, and intake completion; quick Prior History View; configurable Common Intake; correspondence templates; and extensive contact-log management including narratives, searching, editing, and overdue tracking. It also covers multidisciplinary team collaboration, the Legal Actions Framework, eligibility determination with Title IV-E examples, and key background child/family record data such as special cautions, physical characteristics, medical/educational info, gang affiliations, and account transactions.

children.title: 1.3 Referral to Recommendation (Intake)
Intake Narrative
children.summary: Describes intake narrative capture during child welfare intake. The intake worker records free-form, rich-text details from the reporter, including situation, participants and their roles, allegations, and other relevant information. It contrasts narrative capture with strict structured forms and notes browser spell checker use if needed. Also defines intake participant roles used in the process, including reporter, parent/guardian, child, alleged victim, and alleged maltreater, and explains that roles differ from relationships.

children.title: Participant
children.summary: This section explains intake participant roles in child welfare intake. It defines how intake workers assign roles to people involved, such as reporter, parent/guardian, child, alleged victim, and alleged maltreater. It notes that a participant can have multiple roles and that roles differ from relationships. It also describes the reporter role, including mandated reporters, required contact details, possible anonymity, and that reporters may be family, neighbors, or even the child.

children.title: Intake Types
Child Protection Services
children.summary: Explains child welfare intake participant roles and intake types, then defines Child Protective Services (CPS) intake. CPS is created when a report alleges or suspects child abuse or neglect, including harm or threat of harm by a parent/caregiver in home or provider settings. The section emphasizes front-door safety/urgency questions, criteria for determining response priority, and the goal of consistent intake decisions that help manage limited investigative resources while protecting children.

children.title: Family Services
children.summary: Explains Cúram child welfare intake handling for family-related services. Describes Family Services (FS) intakes, created when a family seeks help for difficult circumstances affecting one or more children; these may involve in-home support or temporary voluntary out-of-home placement and require evaluation, goal-setting, and service planning. Also covers Information and Referral (I&R) intakes, used to provide information or referrals to community services like food, shelter, clothing, or counseling, typically without direct agency services.

children.title: Information and Referral
children.summary: This section explains Information and Referral (I&R) intakes in child welfare. An I&R intake is opened when the agency receives a request for information and/or referral to community services such as food, clothing, shelter, or counseling. The agency responds by directing the requester to appropriate outside providers rather than usually offering direct services. The section sits alongside intake assessment content and contrasts I&R with Family Services and child protection intake types.

children.title: Inter-jurisdictional Intake
children.summary: Explains Inter-jurisdictional (IJ) Intake, created when a child services agency receives a referral from another jurisdiction and a child welfare case is transferred between legal authorities (e.g., provinces, states, counties, municipalities). Describes intake recommendation workflow: gather participant/allegation details, validate if intake type changed, recommend screen in/out, and possible supervisor approval, rework, or override. Also introduces the recommendation-to-response/investigation phase after screening in, including reviewing referral details, deciding on a new vs existing investigation, and contacting/logging key participants.

children.title: Intake Recommendation
children.summary: Explains intake recommendation workflow for child services, including inter-jurisdictional intakes, validating whether the intake type changed, and using policy, best practices, and assessment tools to recommend screen in or screen out. Notes that a supervisor may approve, return for rework, or override the recommendation. Also introduces the transition from screened-in referrals to investigation, where the agency reviews the referral, decides whether to start a new investigation or link an existing one, and contacts family and other participants.

Explains how intake workers classify incoming child welfare requests into four intake types: CPS for alleged/suspected abuse or neglect, FS for families seeking services, IJ for transfers between jurisdictions, and I&R for information/referrals. Defines CPS intake criteria, including safety/urgency, allegation details, and optional screening/response assessments. Describes FS intakes as service-oriented, sometimes involving voluntary out-of-home placement and case planning. I&R intakes usually direct people to community services rather than providing direct agency services.

Describes the CCS intake/referral process for child welfare reports. Intake workers capture reporter narrative and participant roles, then classify the intake as CPS, FS, IJ, or I&R and make an initial screening/response recommendation, optionally for supervisor approval. Covers intake narrative capture, participant roles (reporter, parent/guardian, child, alleged victim/maltreater, etc.), and key intake type definitions. CPS intakes require allegations and may use optional screening/response assessments; FS cases are service-oriented; I&R routes people to community services.

children.title: 1.4 Recommendation to Response (Investigation)
Review Referral
children.summary: Explains the review referral step in the investigation process after an intake is screened in. The investigator reviews the originating referral details to decide whether to start a new investigation or link the case to an existing intake. This section sits within the broader recommendation-to-response workflow and follows intake screening/approval, before contacting the family and logging contacts.

children.title: Contact Family
children.summary: Describes the investigation step where, after an intake is accepted, the investigator attempts to locate and contact alleged victims, parents/guardians, collaterals, and sometimes the alleged maltreater. It notes that contact management is used to log interactions and that agencies may require face-to-face contact with key participants within specified timeframes. Useful for navigation if looking for investigation contact procedures, participant outreach, or contact logging requirements.

children.title: Participants
children.summary: This section defines investigation participants as all individuals directly or indirectly involved in a case. It explains that intake participants may be removed or new ones added during investigation, and that a person can have multiple roles (e.g., child, parent/guardian, collateral contact, alleged victim, alleged maltreater). It also distinguishes roles from relationships, which describe reciprocal family connections. The section begins the investigation workflow by covering safety assessment, safety planning, allegation disposition, and risk assessment.

children.title: Assess Safety
children.summary: This section explains the investigation phase steps: contacting alleged victims to assess immediate child safety, identifying safety threats, and creating a safety plan with the family when needed. It distinguishes safety from risk, noting children are unsafe when a threat requires removal to protect them. It also covers disposing allegations using interviews and corroborating evidence, and assessing future risk using optional agency instruments.

children.title: Dispose Allegations
children.summary: This section explains allegation disposition in an investigation. The investigator determines whether allegations are valid by interviewing the alleged victim, alleged maltreater if appropriate, parents/guardians, and collateral contacts, and by corroborating evidence such as law enforcement, medical, or school records. It follows safety assessment and precedes risk assessment, serving as the findings stage used to decide the outcome of the allegations.

children.title: Assess Risk
children.summary: This section explains the investigation workflow around participant interviews, safety assessment, allegation disposition, and risk assessment. It defines investigation participants and notes roles/relationships may change during the case. For safety, the investigator contacts alleged victims, evaluates immediate danger, and may create a safety plan with the family to keep the child in the home. For allegations, the investigator uses interviews and corroborating evidence to determine validity. Risk assessment evaluates future abuse/neglect risk, often using optional jurisdictional instruments.

children.title: Determine Response
children.summary: Explains how investigators determine the next response after assessment. Using risk level, family capacity, and allegation dispositions, they decide whether to transition the family to an ongoing case or close the investigation with no further action. The section then introduces removal to return: when a child’s living situation is unsafe, the agency may remove the child by court order, voluntary agreement, or emergency, with court follow-up often required. It also covers recording removal details, legal actions, and multiple removal episodes.

Covers the investigation phase after an intake is screened in. The investigator reviews the referral to decide whether to start a new investigation or link to an existing intake, then contacts alleged victims, parents/guardians, collaterals, and possibly the alleged maltreater, logging all contacts. It defines investigation participants and their roles/relationships, explains safety assessment and safety planning, allegation disposition using interviews and corroborating evidence, and risk assessment for future harm, including optional agency tools.

children.title: 1.5 Removal to Return
Remove Child
children.summary: Describes child removal in CCS after an unsafe situation is found. Covers initial removal from home or via formal voluntary agreement, notes that a child may have multiple removal episodes, and explains that removals can be court-ordered, voluntary, or emergency-based. Highlights recording removal initiation details (date, reason), removal end/discharge details, prior living arrangement, and legal actions supporting the decision. Also introduces removal types and the broader removal-to-return process.

children.title: Removal Types
children.summary: This section explains child removal in the investigation-to-placement process. It covers when a child may be removed for safety reasons, including court-ordered, voluntary, or emergency removals, and notes that emergency removals often require later court approval. It also describes what the caseworker records: removal date, reasons, type, narrative details, prior living arrangement, and related legal actions. It introduces “Remove Child” and “Removal Types” as part of CCS support for managing removal and discharge details.

children.title: Identify Provider
Placements
children.summary: Explains child welfare placement concepts for out-of-home care. Covers types of removals leading to placement, including court-ordered care/protection actions and voluntary relinquishment/placement agreements. Describes placement service providers as licensed agencies or carers identified and maintained by agencies, with rates managed through provider management. Emphasizes choosing the least restrictive setting by considering the child’s support network, strengths/needs, caretaker skills, and permanency prospects to minimize placement moves. Lists common placement settings such as kin, community, foster, treatment foster, and group/residential care.

children.title: Creating a Placement Request
children.summary: Explains how a caseworker creates a placement request for a child, including requests for known or unknown placement resources and child needs/characteristics. Describes provider/resource worker roles in handling placement management. Covers kith/kin placements as preferred, least intrusive options and the need to vet new kin/kith resources with interviews and background checks. Also outlines the goal of reunification or permanency, noting out-of-home placements are intended to be short term and that discharge can occur through return home or finalized adoption.

children.title: Kith/Kin Placements
children.summary: Explains kith/kin placements in child services. Kith are non-familial but emotionally bonded contacts; kin are relatives. These placements are preferred because they are usually least intrusive, but must be assessed as safe and appropriate. New resources must be vetted first through interviews, background checks, and review of factors like relationship to the child, criminal history, and home conditions. The section also ties placements to the broader goal of returning children home or establishing permanency, including discharge for return home or finalized adoption.

Explains how children enter out-of-home care through court-ordered removals, voluntary relinquishment/placement agreements, or other configurable removal types. Defines placement service providers as licensed carers or agencies maintained by child welfare organizations, with provider registration and payments handled through Provider Management. Notes that placement decisions should favor the least restrictive setting, using the child’s support network, needs, caregiver skills, and permanency prospects to minimize moves. Lists common settings: kin/relative, kith/community, foster, treatment foster, and group/residential care.

children.title: Return Home or Establish Permanency
children.summary: This section explains the goal of child placement services: either safely return the child home or establish permanency. It says out-of-home placements are meant to be short term, and discharge ends both placement services and the removal record. It identifies two discharge reasons: return home when risk is low and the issues causing removal have been addressed, and adoption finalized after termination of parental rights and completion of adoption proceedings.

Covers the removal-to-return workflow for unsafe child situations: when and how a child is removed (court-ordered, voluntary, or emergency), required court approval after emergency removal, and what CCS records (dates, reasons, type, narrative, prior living arrangement, linked legal actions). Describes removal episodes, placement provider identification and least-restrictive placement decisions, including kin/kith and foster or residential options. Ends with discharge goals: return home when safe or adoption finalized after parental rights are terminated.

children.title: 1.6 Outcome Management
Assessment and Planning
children.summary: Outcome Management supports assessment-driven planning for children and families. Agencies can configure multiple assessment types using IEG, with results determined by CDA or CER. Assessment findings help identify issues, prioritize urgent factors, and focus on the most important needs. Based on this, caseworkers create outcome plans that may be legal court documents, involve parents/guardians, assign clear responsibilities, and are reviewed and updated regularly. The planning process also identifies needed services from the CPM services registry or system recommendations.

children.title: Outcome Plan
children.summary: This section explains Outcome Management and the Outcome Plan process for child and family services. It covers using assessment results to identify needs, prioritize issues, and build a client-centered plan with clear tasks, responsibilities, and selected services. It notes that parents/guardians may sign off, legal authorities may review plans, and plans are monitored and updated as circumstances change. It also mentions service selection from the CPM services registry or system recommendations.

children.title: Managing Planned Activities
children.summary: This section explains outcome management for planned activities, focusing on monitoring and maintaining services after a plan is created. It covers adjusting services based on child and family needs, tracking participation and effectiveness, ensuring provider quality and payment, and supporting case reviews. It also notes that caseworkers regularly review outcome plans, may evaluate placements during reviews, and can configure when the first and subsequent reviews occur.

children.title: Monitoring Client Progress/Case Reviews
children.summary: Explains child welfare case reviews for monitoring client progress on outcome plans. Caseworkers regularly review goals, objectives, services, and actions, and can modify plan components based on the client’s progress. If the child was in placement during the review period, the caseworker may also assess current and past placements, including disruptions, positives, and the current removal episode. Outcome plan types can be configured for the first review timing and subsequent review frequency.

Outcome Management describes a client-centered process for improving outcomes for children and families through assessment, outcome planning, managed activities, and progress monitoring. Agencies can configure multiple assessments using IEG with results from CDA or CER, then prioritize urgent factors to identify needs. Caseworkers build outcome plans with clear tasks, responsibilities, parent/guardian participation, possible legal review, and regular updates as circumstances change. Services may be chosen from the CPM services registry or system recommendations.

children.title: 1.7 Ongoing Case Management
Ongoing Case Participants
children.summary: This section outlines who may participate in ongoing cases (child, parents/guardians, family members, other appropriate persons) and focuses on inter-jurisdictional (IJ) agreements for cases crossing jurisdictional lines. It explains why IJ cooperation is needed, gives examples such as kinship placement, home studies, services, and family relocation, and notes CCS support for incoming/outgoing IJ requests and templates. It also introduces adoption cases, including when they can be created and how placement details are shared with ongoing cases.

children.title: Inter-jurisdictional
children.summary: Explains inter-jurisdictional (IJ) agreements for ongoing child welfare and adoption cases. Agencies use IJ agreements when a child moves between jurisdictions or adoption may occur elsewhere, ensuring the child receives equivalent protection, services, and possible return to the original jurisdiction. Gives examples such as kin placements, cross-jurisdiction home studies/supervision, specialized services, and families relocating. Notes CCS supports incoming/outgoing IJ requests, templates, and coordination by specialized staff. Also briefly introduces adoption cases and their relationship to ongoing cases.

Introduces ongoing case management as the operational and logistical management of children being looked after by the agency. This section sits after visitation plans, planned activity monitoring, and case review processes, emphasizing how caseworkers maintain services, placements, and review cycles over time. It serves as the high-level entry point for day-to-day management of open child welfare cases.

children.title: 1.8 Adoption
Participants
children.summary: This section explains adoption participants in child services. It says children can be selected as adoption participants to support finding the most appropriate stable, permanent placement, including single-child and sibling-group adoptions. It also outlines the adoption process: creating an adoption case, managing outcome plans, recruitment activities, pre-adoptive conferences, and assessing prospective adoptive families. Prospective adoptive persons are managed as providers, and recruitment activity details such as type, location, date, child targeted, and interested parents can be recorded.

children.title: Adoption Process
children.summary: Adoption cases begin when a child is removed from the ongoing case after parental rights are terminated or another legal authority indicates adoption. The adoption caseworker creates a new case to manage adoption placement and finalization, including outcome planning, recruitment activities, pre-adoptive conferences, and assessment of prospective adoptive families. Interested adopters are managed as providers. The section also notes documenting recruitment events and selecting single or sibling-group placements best suited to promote stability and permanency.

children.title: Identifying Prospective Families
Initiating Recruitment Activities
children.summary: This section explains how adoption recruitment activities are documented in CCS. It notes that agencies use recruitment events such as web and media campaigns to publicize children available for adoption. The adoption caseworker can record the activity type, location, and date, identify the child or children targeted, and capture whether prospective adoptive parents expressed interest. Open this node if you need details on tracking adoption recruitment efforts and related documentation.

children.title: Providing Placement Recommendation
children.summary: This section explains how an adoption caseworker records placement recommendations for prospective adoptive families evaluated for a pre-adoptive conference. It covers the available recommendation outcomes (Yes, No, Withdrawn), recording reasons and priority rankings, capturing family responses and observations, and how supervisors use this information to make final placement decisions. It also briefly introduces adoption subsidy support, including jurisdiction-specific financial or in-kind assistance and recertification, maintained separately from the adoption case.

children.title: Capturing Family Responses
children.summary: Explains how CCS captures family responses during a pre-adoptive conference, including recording observations about each prospective adoptive family’s participation and interactions with each child. It also describes related adoption workflow steps: caseworker placement recommendations (Yes, No, Withdrawn, with reasons and priority), supervisor placement decisions based on gathered information, and the option for additional contacts if needed. The section closes with an overview of adoption subsidy support for special-needs adoptions, including agreements, recertification, and configurable jurisdiction-specific rules.

This section explains how adoption caseworkers identify prospective adoptive families by searching for persons managed as providers in CPM and selecting those suitable for a pre-adoptive conference. It also notes that adoption recruitment activities can be documented in CCS, including the activity type, location, date, intended child or children, and whether prospective parents expressed interest. Open this node for details on finding families and tracking recruitment efforts.

children.title: Capturing Placement Decisions
children.summary: This section explains how the pre-adoption case supervisor records final placement decisions using caseworker recommendations, observations, and priority ratings. It notes that multiple positive recommendations may exist for a child or sibling group, and additional contacts can be arranged if needed before deciding. It also mentions that CCS can be configured for different agency adoption processes. The page transitions into adoption subsidy, describing financial or in-kind support for special-needs adoptions, agreement terms, recertification, and a sample subsidy process maintained separately from the adoption case.

children.title: Adoption Subsidy
children.summary: This section describes adoption subsidy support in CCS. It explains that jurisdictions may provide financial or in-kind assistance to adoptive families for children with special needs, based on a formal agreement that remains in effect while the child is alive, in the home, and under the age of majority. The subsidy includes periodic recertification to confirm continued eligibility. CCS provides a sample subsidy implementation maintained separately from the adoption case so it stays active after adoption activities end, and it is configurable via Dynamic Programs.

This section explains when and how an adoption case is created for a child in care, especially when return home is not feasible or parental rights are terminated. It covers adoption participants, managing single-child or sibling-group cases, outcome planning, recruitment activities, pre-adoptive conferences, and identifying/profiling prospective adoptive families as providers. It also describes recording placement recommendations and final decisions, plus adoption subsidy support for special-needs adoptions, including recertification and separate case handling.

children.title: 1.9 Performance Indicators
Dashboards
Operational Dashboards
children.summary: This section describes Cúram’s performance indicator dashboards for child welfare agencies. It covers operational dashboards included with the solution, without extra licensing: Initial Contact Timelines and Ongoing Contact Timeliness, both focused on meeting configurable contact-timeframe standards for child safety and worker performance. It also introduces additional licensed dashboards, such as Recurrence of Maltreatment, for evaluating recidivism and other evidence-based measures. Useful for readers comparing reporting options, benchmark compliance, and configurable dashboard metrics.

children.title: Additional Dashboards
children.summary: Additional reporting dashboards are available with extra licensing. They support advanced evaluation using evidence-based metrics such as recidivism, placements, and other international guideline measures. Cúram notes these dashboards are designed to assess child welfare outcomes and agency performance, extending beyond the standard operational dashboards. The section introduces “Recurrence of Maltreatment,” which tracks whether a child who previously experienced substantiated or indicated abuse/neglect experiences another substantiated or indicated incident within a defined time frame.

Cúram’s Dashboards section introduces performance indicators for child welfare agencies, covering graphical reports and configurable metrics for safety, well-being, permanency, and agency/worker performance. It distinguishes operational dashboards included with the solution and no extra license—Initial Contact Timelines and Ongoing Contact Timeliness—from additional licensed dashboards for more advanced evaluation. The section highlights configurable timeframes and standards, and describes Recurrence of Maltreatment, which tracks repeat substantiated or indicated abuse/neglect within a defined period.

Cúram’s Performance Indicators section describes reporting tools—graphical reports, dashboards, and analytics—for child welfare agencies to track safety, well-being, permanency, and agency/worker performance. It notes that timeframes and other parameters are configurable. The section distinguishes included operational dashboards, which require no extra license, such as Initial Contact Timelines and Ongoing Contact Timeliness, from additional licensed dashboards. It also introduces Recurrence of Maltreatment, which measures repeat substantiated or indicated abuse/neglect within a defined period.

children.title: 1.10 Scenarios
Child Protection Services Scenario
Participants
children.summary: Lists the participants in the Child Protection Services scenario. Includes Daniel Walker (14 months), his mother Laura Gibson, her boyfriend Bob Jackson, neighbor Deborah Turner, paternal relatives Amy, Jerry, and Bethany Walker, plus agency staff Joe Hall (Intake Worker), Betty Nelson (Investigator), John Lee (Ongoing Caseworker), and Veronica Castillo (Eligibility Worker). This section identifies who is involved in the scenario and their roles, helping orient the child protection case setup.

children.title: Intake - Child Protection
children.summary: Introduces the child protection services scenario and its participants, then begins an intake case for 14-month-old Daniel Walker. Daniel lives with his mother Laura and her boyfriend Bob; his father Mark is in a halfway house for substance abuse. Laura and Bob have not returned home after partying, leaving Daniel with neighbor Deborah, who notices bruises on his cheek and chin and is concerned about Laura’s behavior. This section sets up suspected neglect/abuse and the intake context.

children.title: Investigation - Child Protection
children.summary: Child Protection investigation workflow for Daniel Walker: intake is screened in after allegations of physical abuse. Investigator Betty Nelson contacts the neighbor, attempts to reach the mother and father, documents contacts, photographs injuries, and arranges a medical exam that finds old fractures and prior abuse. A safety assessment determines the child is unsafe and he is removed to emergency foster care. Betty explores placement with aunt Amy and begins relative foster home licensing. After further interviews, allegations are disposed and the case is transferred to ongoing case management.

children.title: Ongoing
children.summary: Ongoing case management for Daniel Walker after substantiated abuse and emergency removal. Daniel is placed with aunt Amy, who becomes an approved relative foster placement, while ongoing worker John Lee develops an outcome plan with Mark, Amy, and service providers. The section covers supervised visitation, drug testing, counseling, employment and housing goals, regular home visits and contact logs, and Laura’s lack of participation. It also includes 6- and 12-month court reviews, progress toward reunification with Mark, and termination efforts regarding Laura’s parental rights.

Child Protection Services scenario overview. Lists participants: 14-month-old Daniel Walker; mother Laura Gibson; boyfriend Bob Jackson; neighbor Deborah Turner; paternal relatives Amy, Jerry, Bethany Walker; and agency staff Joe Hall, Betty Nelson, John Lee, Veronica Castillo. Intake begins after Deborah reports bruises and neglect concerns. Investigation finds evidence of physical abuse, unsafe home conditions, and Daniel is removed to emergency foster care, later placed with aunt Amy. Ongoing casework covers service planning, supervised visits, drug/alcohol testing, court reviews at 6 and 12 months, reunification efforts with Mark, and Laura’s lack of participation.

children.title: Family Services Scenario
Participants
children.summary: This section lists the “Family Services Scenario” participants and introduces the intake case. Participants are Nick McKesson (14), his mother Anna, father Larry, intake worker Joe Hall, investigation worker Betty Nelson, and ongoing caseworker John Lee. The excerpt describes family stress: Nick’s school problems, isolation, financial hardship after Larry loses his job, Anna working extra, and concern Nick may run away. Joe Hall records the intake, confirms no clear abuse/neglect, and screens the case in for Family Services.

children.title: Intake - Family Services
children.summary: Intake - Family Services describes a family stress referral: Nick McKesson, 14, is withdrawn, skipping homework, refusing school, and had a nighttime incident on the roof. His mother Anna reports financial strain after Larry lost his job, mortgage arrears, and lack of local family support. Intake worker Joe Hall records participant and location information, calms Anna, finds no clear abuse or neglect, but screens the case in for Family Services due to immediate family stress and risk concerns.

children.title: Assessment - Family Services
children.summary: Family services assessment and outcome planning case study for the McKesson family. Betty Nelson investigates school truancy, food insecurity, foreclosure, parental unemployment/underemployment, and possible alcohol misuse. She coordinates with supervisor, school, SHS, and DWP/JobCentre Plus, helping secure food and benefits support. The case is transferred to a 3rd sector agency. A multidisciplinary team then creates an outcome plan focused on Nick’s low self-esteem, school performance, tutoring, counseling, and family sessions, while supporting Anna’s work and Larry’s training/job search.

children.title: Outcome Management Planning - Family Services
children.summary: Family Services outcome management planning follows an assessment of the McKesson family’s crisis: foreclosure, food insecurity, Nick’s school absences and stress, and Larry’s unstable employment and alcohol use. Betty connects the family to school-home support, DWP/job services, and benefit help, then transfers the case to a 3rd sector agency. John Lee convenes an MDT with JobCentre Plus, SHS, and school staff, completes strengths/needs assessments, and creates a plan focused on Nick’s schooling, tutoring, counseling, family sessions, and employment/training support for the parents.

Family Services Scenario follows the McKesson family’s crisis and service response. Nick, 14, is withdrawn, skipping school, and had a roof incident; his parents Anna and Larry face job loss, foreclosure, food insecurity, and no local support. Intake worker Joe Hall screens the case in for Family Services. Investigation worker Betty Nelson assesses the home, school attendance, and family stress, coordinates food, school, benefits, and DWP support, then transfers the case to a 3rd sector agency. Ongoing caseworker John Lee leads MDT planning around Nick’s schooling, counseling, and family/employment support.

children.title: Information and Referral Scenario
Participants
children.summary: This section lists participants in two examples: an information-and-referral case and an inter-jurisdictional child welfare placement. It names the people involved, their roles, ages, and relationships: Elizabeth Farman (6 months), her mother Jennifer, intake worker Joe Hall; and John Taylor (14), his aunt Emily Smith, intake worker Joe Hall, ongoing worker John Lee, and sending-worker Maggie Friedman. It supports understanding who is involved in intake, referral, and cross-jurisdiction placement processing.

children.title: Intake
children.summary: This section describes intake examples and how information is recorded and routed. It includes an Information & Referral intake, where a mother seeks child care and the intake worker refers her to a licensing agency and records the referral details. It also covers an inter-jurisdictional intake, where a foster child in Ontario may be placed with an aunt in another zone; the receiving agency screens in the intake, opens an ongoing case, and completes placement-related checks without launching an investigation.

This section illustrates two intake examples: an Information & Referral case where Jennifer Farman seeks child care for her 6-month-old Elizabeth and intake worker Joe Hall refers her to a licensing agency and records an I&R intake; and an inter-jurisdictional child welfare placement where 14-year-old John Taylor may be placed with his aunt Emily Smith in Toronto, prompting a screened-in IJ intake, creation of an ongoing case, and placement checks by receiving and sending workers.

children.title: Inter-jurisdictional Scenario
Participants
children.summary: This section lists scenario participants for child services examples. It names people involved in an Information & Referral case (Elizabeth Farman, Jennifer Farman, Joe Hall) and an inter-jurisdictional placement case (John Taylor, Emily Smith, Joe Hall, John Lee, Maggie Friedman). It helps identify the roles of intake and ongoing workers, family members, and the children involved in referral and placement processes.

children.title: Inter-jurisdictional Process
children.summary: This section explains an inter-jurisdictional child welfare placement process. A 14-year-old in foster care in northern Ontario may be placed with an aunt in Toronto, involving a sending worker, a receiving intake worker, and an ongoing worker. It shows that the receiving CAS records the intake as inter-jurisdictional, screens it in without an investigation, opens an ongoing case, and completes interviews, site visits, and background checks before deciding on the placement.

This section presents two example scenarios in child services: an Information & Referral intake and an inter-jurisdictional placement process. The IJ example follows 14-year-old John Taylor, whose caseworker in northern Ontario requests placement with his aunt in Toronto. The receiving CAS records the intake as inter-jurisdictional, screens it in without investigation, opens an ongoing case, and conducts interviews, site visits, and background checks before deciding on placement.

children.title: Adoption Scenario
Participants
children.summary: Lists the adoption scenario participants and roles: James and Christy Stance (siblings), Jeremy Johnson (adoption supervisor), Geoffrey Wilkins (adoption caseworker), and two prospective adoptive couples, Mark and Mary Jones and Jerry and Sara Matthews. The scenario centers on Geoffrey managing a single sibling adoption case after TPR, conducting pre-adoptive conferences and visits, and ultimately recommending the Jones family, who are approved to adopt both children.

children.title: Adoption Process
children.summary: Illustrative adoption workflow for two siblings, James and Christy Stance, after TPR: create a single adoption case, verify legal/removal/placement info, upload a photo listing, receive interest from two families, run pre-adoptive conferences, schedule visits, confirm mutual interest, recommend the Jones family, obtain supervisor approval, and complete adoption. Also notes possible adoption subsidy eligibility. Pages 43–44 are legal notices, copyright, trademarks, privacy policy, and sample code disclaimers.

Illustrative adoption scenario for siblings James Stance (14) and Christy Stance (7) after TPR. Geoffrey Wilkins, under supervisor Jeremy Johnson, creates one adoption case, verifies legal/removal/placement data, posts a photo listing, receives interest from the Joneses and Matthewses, conducts pre-adoptive conferences and visits, and recommends the pre-approved Mark and Mary Jones, who adopt both children. Notes possible adoption subsidy eligibility. Pages 43–44 are mainly legal notices, copyright, trademarks, privacy policy, and sample code disclaimers.

Section 1.10 presents five child welfare scenarios illustrating system workflows: child protection, family services, information & referral, inter-jurisdictional placement, and adoption. It follows intake, investigation, ongoing case management, safety assessment, placement, reunification, and court review processes through named examples (e.g., Daniel Walker, Nick McKesson, John Taylor, James and Christy Stance). The final pages are legal notices, copyright, trademarks, privacy policy, and sample code disclaimers.

Child Services Business Guide overviews CCS for child welfare agencies: intake, investigation, removal/placement, reunification, ongoing case management, outcome management, and adoption. It highlights caseworker tools such as role-based home pages, prior history, common intake, correspondence, contact logging, MDT collaboration, legal actions, background/special caution data, budgeting, and eligibility/funding. It also introduces performance indicators/dashboards and uses scenarios to illustrate child protection, family services, I&R, inter-jurisdictional placement, and adoption workflows. Closing pages contain legal notices, trademarks, privacy, and sample-code disclaimers.
